title: Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill honors the late boxer Muhammad Ali by giving him a Congressional Gold Medal, which is a special award from Congress. The medal recognizes Muhammad Ali for his boxing achievements and his work helping others around the world. Since Muhammad Ali has passed away, the medal will be given to his wife. The government can also make bronze copies of the medal to sell to people who want to buy them. The money from selling these copies will help pay for making the medals.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned the following facts about Muhammad Ali. He was born as Cassius Marcellus Clay Junior on January seventeenth nineteen forty two in Louisville Kentucky. His parents were Cassius Senior and Odessa Clay and he was their first child. Muhammad Ali became one of the most famous athletes of the twentieth century. He created some of the greatest sports memories in the United States. This included winning a gold medal at the nineteen sixty Summer Olympics and lighting the Olympic torch at the nineteen ninety six Summer Olympics. After a successful amateur career where he won two National AAU light heavyweight titles Muhammad Ali became the first professional boxer in history to win the heavyweight title three separate times. On April twenty eighth nineteen sixty seven he lost his boxing title and was banned from competing because he refused to fight in the Vietnam War for religious and moral reasons. However in nineteen seventy one the Supreme Court of the United States unanimously agreed that Muhammad Ali was a conscientious objector which means someone who refuses to fight in wars for moral or religious reasons. His boxing license was given back and he was cleared of any wrongdoing. As an African American and a Muslim living during a time when his civil rights were questioned Muhammad Ali fought against issues of race and religion. He was recognized as one of the champions of the Civil Rights Movement in the United States. Muhammad Ali received many awards for his athletic skills and his support of racial harmony. These awards included the Doctor Martin Luther King Memorial Award the Spirit of America Award the Amnesty International Lifetime Achievement Award the Arthur Ashe Award for Courage the Essence Living Legend Award the Rainbow Coalition Lifetime Achievement Award the XNBA Human Spirit Award the Presidential Citizens Medal and the Presidential Medal of Freedom. Many organizations recognized Muhammad Ali for his achievements both inside and outside the boxing ring. Sports Illustrated named him Sportsman of the Century. GQ magazine named him Athlete of the Century. The British Broadcasting Corporation named him Sports Personality of the Century. The Kentucky Athletic Hall of Fame named him Kentucky Athlete of the Century. The State of Kentucky named him Kentuckian of the Century. The Advertising Club of Louisville named him Louisvillian of the Century. The World Sports Awards of the Century named him Boxer of the Century. The International Boxing Hall of Fame recognized him. Muhlenberg College and Princeton University gave him honorary doctorate degrees which are special degrees given to honor someone. Muhammad Ali received the Otto Hahn Peace Medal in Gold from the United Nations Association of Germany for his work with the United Nations and the Civil Rights Movement in the United States. The California Bicentennial Foundation for the US Constitution selected Muhammad Ali for representing the energy of the Bill of Rights in various high profile activities. Even though he was diagnosed with Parkinsons disease in the early nineteen eighties Muhammad Ali dedicated his life to the cause of universal human rights and freedom. His commitment to equal justice and peace touched the lives of hundreds of thousands of people worldwide. President Jimmy Carter asked Muhammad Ali to meet with African leaders in Tanzania Kenya Nigeria Liberia and Senegal as part of President Carters diplomatic efforts to promote human rights in the nineteen eighties. In nineteen ninety Muhammad Ali traveled to the Middle East to seek the release of United States and British hostages that were being held as human shields in the first Gulf War. Because of his intervention fifteen United States hostages were freed on December second of that year. In nineteen ninety eight Muhammad Ali was chosen as the UN Messenger of Peace. Several Presidents of the United States recognized Muhammad Ali. President George W Bush on November seventeenth two thousand and two called him a man of peace and stated that across the world billions of people know Muhammad Ali as a brave compassionate and charming man and the American people are proud to call Muhammad Ali one of our own. President Bill Clinton stated that Muhammad Ali captured the worlds imagination and its heart and that outside the ring Muhammad Ali has dedicated his life to working for children feeding the hungry supporting his faith and standing up for racial equality. Clinton said Ali always fought for a just and more humane world breaking down barriers here in America and around the world and that there is no telling how many tens of millions of people had their hearts swell with pride and their eyes swell with tears in nineteen ninety six when Muhammad Ali lit the Olympic torch because we know now and forever he is the greatest. President Jimmy Carter called Muhammad Ali Mister International Friendship. President Barack Obama as a Senator had a framed picture of Muhammad Ali hanging in his office and before announcing his intentions to run for President Obama visited with Muhammad Ali at the Ali Center in Louisville Kentucky. Muhammad Ali encouraged humanity through his perseverance and the support of thousands of people. He helped such organizations as the Chicago based adoption agency The Cradle the Make A Wish Foundation the Special Olympics organization Best Buddies and Herbert E Birch Services an organization that runs a school for handicapped children and young adults in addition to a summer camp for children with AIDS. Muhammad Ali and his wife Lonnie were the founding directors of the Muhammad Ali Parkinson Center in Phoenix Arizona and helped raise over fifty million dollars for Parkinsons research. The Centers mission is to provide excellence in treatment research and education for patients and families affected by Parkinsons disease and other movement disorders regardless of their ability to pay. Muhammad Ali was an inspiration to countless individuals with Parkinsons disease including members of the Rock Steady Boxing Foundation in Indianapolis Indiana which was founded to give people with Parkinsons disease hope by improving their quality of life using boxing for fitness. Muhammad Ali was one of the founding members of Athletes for Hope an organization created by a few very successful athletes of exemplary character who have a deep commitment to charitable and community causes. Muhammad Ali also established the Muhammad Ali Center in his hometown of Louisville Kentucky which promotes respect hope and understanding and inspires people everywhere to be as great as they can be. A visitor of the Muhammad Ali Center experiences the hows of Alis life including how he found the courage the dedication and the discipline to become a world champion how he found the conviction to stand up for what he believed and how he turned his passion for excellence in the ring to a passion for peace on the world stage. Like Muhammad Ali himself the Muhammad Ali Center focuses on what brings individuals together not what sets them apart and is a global gathering place to which people can come both online and in person to learn share and celebrate our commonalities as human beings and to formulate ways of advancing humanity. Muhammad Ali helped to provide more than twenty two million aid packets to assist people in need and until the last years of his life he traveled on average more than two hundred days per year for humanitarian causes. Muhammad Ali known simply as the greatest transcended the glamour and glory of being a sports champion to become not only one of the greatest sports figures but one of the greatest role models of our time. On June third two thousand and sixteen Muhammad Ali died at the age of seventy four.

sections.title: Section 3 Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (the temporary leader of the Senate) must make proper arrangements to present a special gold medal on behalf of Congress to honor Muhammad Ali for his contributions to the United States. For this presentation the Secretary of the Treasury (called the Secretary in this law) must create a gold medal with suitable symbols designs and words that the Secretary will decide. After the gold medal is presented to honor Muhammad Ali the medal will be given to his wife Lonnie Ali.

sections.title: Section 4 Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary can make and sell copies of the gold medal in bronze metal at a price that covers the cost of making the medals including labor materials dies use of machinery and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5 Status of Medals
sections.text: Medals made under this law are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one United States Code (a section of federal law about medals). For purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one United States Code all medals made under this law will be considered numismatic items (collectible coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6 Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The government is authorized to charge the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund for any amounts needed to pay for the costs of the medals made under this law. Money received from selling the duplicate bronze medals authorized under section four will be deposited into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
